bins.name: Preserve State Jurisdictional Authority
bins.desc: The filers support a cooperative federalism model that sets federal minimum standards while fully preserving state authority over retail load interconnections and curtailments. They argue FERC should clarify that RTOs may depart from pro-rata curtailment when large loads caused the shortage, but only after exhausting alternatives and only when the affected customers have other supply options.
quotes: FERC should adopt a cooperative federalism model to govern large load interconnections, curtailments, and transmission cost allocations that preserves existing state jurisdiction.
curtailments should protect current customers, especially residential consumers, over new large load customers that cause the resource inadequacy

FERC can and should recognize that RTOs must depart from pro-rata transmission curtailment in the rare circumstances where (1) the large loads have caused the resource inadequacy; and (2) the RTO has (a) exhausted all of its options for avoiding transmission curtailment of customers, and (b) has established that those transmission customers that would be disproportionately affected by the curtailment – new large loads – have options to reduce their need for transmission capacity, including alternative supply options, compensation for demand response, or reduced transmission rates for the interruptible service they are provided.

bins.name: Retain 20 MW Threshold, Aggregate Basis
bins.desc: The filers support the proposed 20 MW threshold and oppose raising it, arguing a lower threshold protects against reliability risks and deters evasion. They also argue the threshold should apply to a customer's aggregate demand and to planned full operational capacity rather than initial or per-point capacity.
quotes: treating new large loads differently from other load is reasonable and necessary to ensure reliable service and just and reasonable rates for existing consumers, especially residential consumers

FERC can and must appropriately treat new large loads attributable to data centers differently from other kinds of load because: (1) data centers are creating unique difficulties for the grid and impacting a uniquely vulnerable market at a uniquely expensive level; and (2) investments in data centers can be speculative and uncertain in nature, increasing the risk of a severe market correction that would create stranded costs.

FERC should stay the course and adopt the proposed 20 MW threshold to define large loads and hybrid facilities because this MW threshold helps protect against reliability concerns from large loads or hybrid facilities by requiring proper interconnection studies that will reduce reliability risks for all electricity consumers.
the threshold 'should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement rather than to a single point of interconnection.'

The definition of large load and hybrid facilities should use the planned full operational capacity, even if the facility is planning to ramp up its operations over time. Otherwise, a new large load or hybrid facility could circumvent the threshold requirement by phasing in its operations.

bins.name: Gross Metering for Hybrid Facilities
bins.desc: The filers support grid-protective injection and withdrawal assumptions for hybrid facilities and recommend separate gross metering of load and generation components. Net metering, they argue, allows hybrid customers to evade their fair share of transmission costs and shifts those costs to other ratepayers.
quotes: FERC should approach injection and withdrawal rights according to grid-protective assumptions that prevent free-riding and regulatory evasion.
the Joint Consumer Advocates recommend that hybrid facilities have their load and generation components metered separately, to ensure fair and accurate cost allocation of transmission costs.

bins.name: Enforce Curtailment Commitments Fully
bins.desc: The filers support expedited interconnection for facilities that commit to curtailment or dispatch, but insist those commitments must be verifiable, enforceable, and subject to penalties. They oppose any caps on the number of curtailment events, arguing such caps shift risk onto other customers who did not receive the expedited treatment.
quotes: any curtailable or dispatchable commitments made by large loads and hybrid facilities must be verifiable, enforceable, and subject to penalties for non-performance. There should not be limits on the number of curtailable events or the number of times for curtailments as some request.

bins.name: 100% Cost Assignment, No Credits
bins.desc: The filers urge FERC to assign 100% of network upgrade costs to the large load or hybrid facility that caused them, using a but-for causation test or a higher-of policy, with no crediting mechanism. They argue the current Mobile-Sierra review of individually negotiated agreements leaves existing customers exposed to mounting cumulative transmission cost increases.
quotes: large load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrade assigned to them through the interconnection studies and that these costs should not be offset through a crediting mechanism.

FERC should revisit a 'but-for' test when determining which costs should be paid 100% by the large load customers, similar to the test traditionally used by FERC for cost allocation for the interconnection of generators.

Without cost allocation reform, existing customers will face mounting transmission costs due to the cumulative effect of a patchwork of individually negotiated interconnection agreements, reviewed under a Mobile-Sierra standard, which comprise a significant portion of current grid usage.

bins.name: Sound Interconnection Studies Required
bins.desc: The filers support comprehensive and technically sound interconnection studies for large loads and hybrid facilities. They argue the 20 MW threshold and aggregate demand basis ensure the study process captures all facilities that pose reliability risks, and that joint studies of hybrid facilities must not compromise study quality.
quotes: Residential ratepayers should not suffer unreliable electricity service or pay unreasonably high rates for inferior, unreliable grid service as a result of the interconnection of large loads that do not offset their demand through the addition of timely, local, and adequate capacity resources.

FERC should stay the course and adopt the proposed 20 MW threshold to define large loads and hybrid facilities because this MW threshold helps protect against reliability concerns from large loads or hybrid facilities by requiring proper interconnection studies that will reduce reliability risks for all electricity consumers.
the threshold 'should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement rather than to a single point of interconnection.'

The definition of large load and hybrid facilities should use the planned full operational capacity, even if the facility is planning to ramp up its operations over time. Otherwise, a new large load or hybrid facility could circumvent the threshold requirement by phasing in its operations.

bins.name: Protect Residential Ratepayers from Cost Shifts
bins.desc: Cost protection for residential ratepayers is the central theme of the filing. The filers oppose any cost socialization or crediting that moves large-load network upgrade costs onto existing customers, and argue data centers' speculative investment nature makes stranded cost risk especially acute.
quotes: Residential ratepayers should not suffer unreliable electricity service or pay unreasonably high rates for inferior, unreliable grid service as a result of the interconnection of large loads that do not offset their demand through the addition of timely, local, and adequate capacity resources.
FERC should adopt a cooperative federalism model to govern large load interconnections, curtailments, and transmission cost allocations that preserves existing state jurisdiction.
treating new large loads differently from other load is reasonable and necessary to ensure reliable service and just and reasonable rates for existing consumers, especially residential consumers

FERC can and must appropriately treat new large loads attributable to data centers differently from other kinds of load because: (1) data centers are creating unique difficulties for the grid and impacting a uniquely vulnerable market at a uniquely expensive level; and (2) investments in data centers can be speculative and uncertain in nature, increasing the risk of a severe market correction that would create stranded costs.
the Joint Consumer Advocates recommend that hybrid facilities have their load and generation components metered separately, to ensure fair and accurate cost allocation of transmission costs.

large load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrade assigned to them through the interconnection studies and that these costs should not be offset through a crediting mechanism.

FERC should revisit a 'but-for' test when determining which costs should be paid 100% by the large load customers, similar to the test traditionally used by FERC for cost allocation for the interconnection of generators.

Without cost allocation reform, existing customers will face mounting transmission costs due to the cumulative effect of a patchwork of individually negotiated interconnection agreements, reviewed under a Mobile-Sierra standard, which comprise a significant portion of current grid usage.

bins.name: Gross Load Basis for Co-located Generation
bins.desc: The filers recommend that hybrid facilities with co-located generation be metered and studied on a gross load basis, not net, to prevent free-riding on the transmission system and subsidization by other customers.
quotes: FERC should approach injection and withdrawal rights according to grid-protective assumptions that prevent free-riding and regulatory evasion.
the Joint Consumer Advocates recommend that hybrid facilities have their load and generation components metered separately, to ensure fair and accurate cost allocation of transmission costs.

bins.name: Curtailment Tied to Causation, Fully Enforceable
bins.desc: The filers support flexible or curtailable interconnection options but insist curtailment must be tied to causal responsibility: loads that caused resource inadequacy should bear curtailment risk first. Any flexibility commitment must be enforceable without event-count caps.
quotes: curtailments should protect current customers, especially residential consumers, over new large load customers that cause the resource inadequacy

FERC can and should recognize that RTOs must depart from pro-rata transmission curtailment in the rare circumstances where (1) the large loads have caused the resource inadequacy; and (2) the RTO has (a) exhausted all of its options for avoiding transmission curtailment of customers, and (b) has established that those transmission customers that would be disproportionately affected by the curtailment – new large loads – have options to reduce their need for transmission capacity, including alternative supply options, compensation for demand response, or reduced transmission rates for the interruptible service they are provided.

any curtailable or dispatchable commitments made by large loads and hybrid facilities must be verifiable, enforceable, and subject to penalties for non-performance. There should not be limits on the number of curtailable events or the number of times for curtailments as some request.

bins.name: Speculative Data Center Investment Risk
bins.desc: The filers flag that investment in new data center loads is more speculative than typical load growth, citing risk of a market correction that would strand network upgrade costs. This risk reinforces their argument that large loads should pay up front and that FERC should not build crediting mechanisms that defer cost recovery.
quotes: FERC can and must appropriately treat new large loads attributable to data centers differently from other kinds of load because: (1) data centers are creating unique difficulties for the grid and impacting a uniquely vulnerable market at a uniquely expensive level; and (2) investments in data centers can be speculative and uncertain in nature, increasing the risk of a severe market correction that would create stranded costs.

bins.name: Mobile-Sierra Review Leaves Cost Gap
bins.desc: The filers argue that individually negotiated transmission security agreements reviewed under the Mobile-Sierra standard provide insufficient consumer protection against the cumulative cost impact of many large load additions. They urge FERC to establish minimum cost allocation standards that address this structural gap.
quotes: Without cost allocation reform, existing customers will face mounting transmission costs due to the cumulative effect of a patchwork of individually negotiated interconnection agreements, reviewed under a Mobile-Sierra standard, which comprise a significant portion of current grid usage.
